description: Baseline published-read benchmark on current branch state before new optimizations.
Replace the per-request schema Effect.Cache path with a plain memoized schema promise and skip SQL metrics/schema timing work on untraced GraphQL requests.

Tried avoiding unconditional request cloning in the HTTP router and skipping GraphQL credential header rewriting when the request has no auth. Benchmark improved, but the broader test suite failed badly, so revert.
Moved query limit enforcement from Yoga onParams to onValidate to reuse the parsed AST instead of reparsing, but this version both regressed the benchmark and introduced TypeScript errors.

Tried a published-only partial row deserializer that eagerly parses just _published_snapshot and prefetched JSON fields instead of every JSON-like column. Checks passed, but benchmark regressed versus the current best.
Tried a single-pass DAST reference extractor to reduce repeated structured-text tree walks, but this version regressed the benchmark and changed extractAllBlockIds ordering in tests.

Tried slimming JSON link prefetch payloads by dropping _published_snapshot for safe non-localized simple linked fields so prefetched author/category records avoid snapshot merging. Checks passed, but it was still slightly slower than the current best.

Tried skipping GraphQL credential detection when the request has no auth header, but that changed local-dev no-writeKey semantics (published-only instead of implicit admin) and blew up many tests despite a small benchmark win.
Tried inlining the no-writeKey /graphql credential type to 'admin' to avoid the async actor lookup in local dev, but it caused a large benchmark regression.
Disabled Yoga's built-in logging to see if the default plugin stack was adding request overhead. Checks passed, but it was still slower than the current best.
Disabled Yoga's landing page plugin to trim non-query features from the handler stack, but the benchmark regressed.
Tried skipping CORS header synthesis when a request has no Origin header so same-process benchmarks avoid an extra response wrapper, but it regressed badly.
Re-ran the current best state to gauge noise. The no-change rerun came in slower than the kept 44.186ms result, so keep the existing best as the branch tip.

Tried a standard-POST JSON fast path that parses the body once and calls Yoga's getResultForParams directly, bypassing the full HTTP adapter for common GraphQL requests. Checks passed, but it was still slower than the current best.

Converted several hot published-field resolvers from async functions to sync-or-promise returns so prefetched links and structured text avoid an extra Promise wrapper on the fast path, but the benchmark still regressed.

First pass at a generic published-read SQL fast path for simple singleton/list/meta/link queries. It executed correctly for summary/page-style queries, but this version added TypeScript errors and regressed due to fallback overhead on unsupported operations.
Refined the /graphql auth shortcut to preserve no-writeKey local-dev admin semantics while skipping credential lookup for unauthenticated requests, but it still regressed versus the current best.
Tried skipping the initial x-request-id request clone for /graphql only while preserving it for other routes, but it still regressed relative to the best kept handler-only optimization.

Tried moving query-limit enforcement to Yoga's parse hook so the already-parsed AST could be reused instead of reparsing the query string, but this version introduced a missing-type import and still regressed versus the best state.
Built a narrower published-read fast path with a cheap textual support gate for simple scalar/link/list/meta queries. It nearly matched the best benchmark, but TypeScript rejected the first draft.

Retried the narrow fast path with fixed types. It preserved the benchmark shape but returned draft scalar values for published reads because it selected live columns instead of snapshot values, so tests failed.

Tried caching query-limit analysis results by query string to avoid reparsing identical benchmark queries on every request. The idea compiled conceptually but this draft used the wrong error array type and still benchmarked worse than the best state.

Cached GraphQL AST-derived selection planning in sqlite-json-prefetch (selected field names, immediate selection maps, and link prefetch specs) keyed by root field + query text. One run hit 42.227ms, but an immediate no-change rerun landed at 54.340ms, so the apparent win did not reproduce cleanly and I’m discarding for now.

Tried a proper published SQLite JSON fast path that reads selected scalar/link fields from _published_snapshot and builds nested JSON with json_object/json_group_array for simple published roots. Correctness checks passed, but the benchmark still regressed versus the current best and changed the filter-by-category response shape enough to warrant caution.

Built a narrower SQLite JSON fast path that only handles unfiltered published singleton/list/meta roots and leaves filtered/deep queries on Yoga. It was very close to the current best, but the first draft had two trivial TypeScript errors.

Implemented a narrow SQLite JSON fast path for unfiltered published singleton/list/meta roots only, using _published_snapshot plus json_object/json_group_array and falling back to Yoga for filtered/deep/SEO queries. It passed checks and was directionally close, but still came in slightly slower than the current best.

Cached a compiled narrow SQLite JSON fast path for repeated unfiltered published roots. Benchmark looked better, but manual verification showed the list fast path ignored variable-driven `first` and returned 20 rows instead of 12, so discard as incorrect.

Retried the cached narrow SQLite JSON fast path with variable-aware limits/orderBy. Checks passed, but manual verification showed boolean fields were serialized as 0/1 instead of true/false, so discard as response-shape incorrect.

Cache a compiled SQLite JSON fast path for unfiltered published singleton/list/meta roots, with variable-aware first/skip/orderBy handling and correct boolean serialization from _published_snapshot; unsupported filtered/deep/SEO queries still fall back to Yoga.
Extended the cached SQLite JSON fast path to cover inline seo object selections from _published_snapshot, but the added support regressed the suite versus the current best.
Tried running the cached published fast path before query-limit enforcement so supported queries avoid the extra reparse, but it regressed badly and increased variance.

Tried reordering the non-traced HTTP path to attempt the cached published fast path before query-limit enforcement, so supported queries avoid the extra limit-analysis parse. Two confirming runs stayed slower than the current best commit, so discard.

Tried extending the cached SQLite JSON fast path to simple eq-filtered published list/meta roots using json_extract(_published_snapshot, '$.<field>') comparisons. It regressed badly and changed the filter_by_category response shape/size, so discard.

Precomputed constant/default orderBy SQL inside the cached published fast-path plan so supported list queries skip compileOrderBy at request time. Checks passed, but it was still slower than the current best commit.
Tried replacing the fast-path JSON decoder with native JSON.parse instead of the shared decodeJsonIfString helper. Despite sounding cheaper, this regressed the benchmark substantially.
